author: John of Damascus: Exposition of the Orthodox Faith
book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: John of Damascus: Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
Concerning the energies in our Lord Jesus Christ.

metadata.author: John of Damascus: Exposition of the Orthodox Faith

ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning the energies in our Lord Jesus Christ.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: We hold, further, that there are two energies in our Lord Jesus Christ.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For He possesses on the one hand, as God and being of like essence with the Father, the divine energy, and, likewise, since He became man and of like essence to us, the energy proper to human nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But observe that energy and capacity for energy, and the product of energy, and the agent of energy, are all different.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Energy is the efficient (δραστική) and essential activity of nature: the capacity for energy is the nature from which proceeds energy: the product of energy is that which is effected by energy: and the agent of energy is the person or subsistence which uses the energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Further, sometimes energy is used in the sense of the product of energy, and the product of energy in that of energy, just as the terms creation and creature are sometimes transposed.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For we say “all creation,” meaning creatures.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Note also that energy is an activity and is energised rather than energises; as Gregory the Theologian says in his thesis concerning the Holy Spirit: “If energy exists, it must manifestly be energised and will not energise: and as soon as it has been energised, it will cease.”

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Life itself, it should be observed, is energy, yea, the primal energy of the living creature and so is the whole economy of the living creature, its functions of nutrition and growth, that is, the vegetative side of its nature, and the movement stirred by impulse, that is, the sentient side, and its activity of intellect and free-will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Energy, moreover, is the perfect realisation of power.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: If, then, we contemplate all these in Christ, surely we must also hold that He possesses human energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The first thought that arises in us is called energy: and it is simple energy not involving any relationship, the mind sending forth the thoughts peculiar to it in an independent and invisible way, for if it did not do so it could not justly be called mind.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Again, the revelation and unfolding of thought by means of articulate speech is said to be energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But this is no longer simple energy that involves no relationship, but it is considered in relation as being composed of thought and speech.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Further, the very relation which he who does anything bears to that which is brought about is energy; and the very thing that is effected is called energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The first belongs to the soul alone, the second to the soul making use of the body, the third to the body animated by mind, and the last is the effect.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the mind sees beforehand what is to be and then performs it thus by means of the body.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And so the hegemony belongs to the soul, for it uses the body as an instrument, leading and restraining it.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But the energy of the body is quite different, for the body is led and moved by the soul.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And with regard to the effect, the touching and handling and, so to speak, the embrace of what is effected, belong to the body, while the figuration and formation belong to the soul.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And so in connection with our Lord Jesus Christ, the power of miracles is the energy of His divinity, while the work of His hands and the willing and the saying, I will, be thou clean, are the energy of His humanity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And as to the effect, the breaking of the loaves, and the fact that the leper heard the “I will,” belong to His humanity, while the multiplication of the loaves and the purification of the leper belong to His divinity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For through both, that is through the energy of the body and the energy of the soul, He displayed one and the same, cognate and equal divine energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For just as we saw that His natures were united and permeate one another, and yet do not deny that they are different but even enumerate them, although we know they are inseparable, so also in connection with the wills and the energies we know their union, and we recognise their difference and enumerate them without introducing separation.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For just as the flesh was deified without undergoing change in its own nature, in the same way also will and energy are deified without transgressing their own proper limits.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For whether He is the one or the other, He is one and the same, and whether He wills and energises in one way or the other, that is as God or as man, He is one and the same.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: We must, then, maintain that Christ has two energies in virtue of His double nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For things that have diverse natures, have also different energies, and things that have diverse energies, have also different natures.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And so conversely, things that have the same nature have also the same energy, and things that have one and the same energy have also one and the same essence, which is the view of the Fathers, who declare the divine meaning.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: One of these alternatives, then, must be true: either, if we hold that Christ has one energy, we must also hold that He has but one essence, or, if we are solicitous about truth, and confess that He has according to the doctrine of the Gospels and the Fathers two essences, we must also confess that He has two energies corresponding to and accompanying them.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For as He is of like essence with God and the Father in divinity, He will be His equal also in energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And as He likewise is of like essence with us in humanity He will be our equal also in energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the blessed Gregory, bishop of Nyssa, says, “Things that have one and the same energy, have also absolutely the same power.”

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For all energy is the effect of power.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But it cannot be that uncreated and created nature have one and the same nature or power or energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if we should hold that Christ has but one energy, we should attribute to the divinity of the Word the passions of the intelligent spirit, viz. fear and grief and anguish.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: If they should say, indeed, that the holy Fathers said in their disputation concerning the Holy Trinity, “Things that have one and the same essence have also one and the same energy, and things which have different essences have also different energies,” and that it is not right to transfer to the dispensation what has reference to matters of theology, we shall answer that if it has been said by the Fathers solely with reference to theology, and if the Son has not even after the incarnation the same energy as the Father, assuredly He cannot have the same essence.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But to whom shall we attribute this, My Father worketh hitherto and I work: and this, What things soever He seeth the Father doing, these also doeth the Son likewise: and this, If ye believe not Me, believe My works: and this, The work which I do bear witness concerning Me: and this, As the Father raised up the dead and quickeneth them, even so the Son quickeneth whom He will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For all these shew not only that He is of like essence to the Father even after the incarnation, but that He has also the same energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And again: if the providence that embraces all creation is not only of the Father and the Holy Spirit, but also of the Son even after the incarnation, assuredly since that is energy, He must have even after the incarnation the same energy as the Father.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if we have learnt from the miracles that Christ has the same essence as the Father, and since the miracles happen to be the energy of God, assuredly He must have even after the incarnation the same energy as the Father.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But, if there is one energy belonging to both His divinity and His humanity, it will be compound, and will be either a different energy from that of the Father, or the Father, too, will have a compound energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if the Father has a compound energy, manifestly He must also have a compound nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if they should say that together with energy is also introduced personality, we shall reply that if personality is introduced along with energy, then the true converse must hold good that energy is also introduced along with personality; and there will be also three energies of the Holy Trinity just as there are three persons or subsistences, or there will be one person and one subsistence just as there is only one energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Indeed, the holy Fathers have maintained with one voice that things that have the same essence have also the same energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But further, if personality is introduced along with energy, those who divine that neither one nor two energies of Christ are to be spoken of, do not maintain that either one or two persons of Christ are to be spoken of.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Take the case of the flaming sword; just as in it the natures of the fire and the steel are preserved distinct, so also are their two energies and their effects.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the energy of the steel is its cutting power, and that of the fire is its burning power, and the cut is the effect of the energy of the steel, and the burn is the effect of the energy of the fire: and these are kept quite distinct in the burnt cut, and in the cut burn, although neither does the burning take place apart from the cut after the union of the two, nor the cut apart from the burning: and we do not maintain on account of the twofold natural energy that there are two flaming swords, nor do we confuse the essential difference of the energies on account of the unity of the flaming sword.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: In like manner also, in the case of Christ, His divinity possesses an energy that is divine and omnipotent while His humanity has an energy such as is our own.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And the effect of His human energy was His taking the child by the hand and drawing her to Himself, while that of His divine energy was the restoring of her to life.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the one is quite distinct from the other, although they are inseparable from one another in theandric energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if, because Christ has one subsistence, He must also have one energy, then, because He has one subsistence, He must also have one essence.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And again: if we should hold that Christ has but one energy, this must be either divine or human, or neither.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if we hold that it is divine we must maintain that He is God alone, stripped of our humanity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And if we hold that it is human, we shall be guilty of the impiety of saying that He is mere man.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And if we hold that it is neither divine nor human, we must also hold that He is neither God nor man, of like essence neither to the Father nor to us.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For it is as a result of the union that the identity in hypostasis arises, but yet the difference between the natures is not done away with.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But since the difference between the natures is preserved, manifestly also the energies of the natures will be preserved.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For no nature exists that is lacking in energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: If Christ our Master has one energy, it must be either created or uncreated; for between these there is no energy, just as there is no nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: If, then, it is created, it will point to created nature alone, but if it is uncreated, it will betoken uncreated essence alone.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For that which is natural must completely correspond with its nature: for there cannot exist a nature that is defective.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But the energy that harmonises with nature does not belong to that which is external: and this is manifest because, apart from the energy that harmonises with nature, no nature can either exist or be known.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For through that in which each thing manifests its energy, the absence of change confirms its own proper nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: If Christ has one energy, it must be one and the same energy that performs both divine and human actions.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But there is no existing thing which abiding in its natural state can act in opposite ways: for fire does not freeze and boil, nor does water dry up and make wet.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: How then could He Who is by nature God, and Who became by nature man, have both performed miracles, and endured passions with one and the same energy?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: If, then, Christ assumed the human mind, that is to say, the intelligent and reasonable soul, undoubtedly He has thought, and will think for ever.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But thought is the energy of the mind: and so Christ, as man, is endowed with energy, and will be so for ever.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Indeed, the most wise and great and holy John Chrysostom says in his interpretation of the Acts, in the second discourse, “One would not err if he should call even His passion action: for in that He suffered all things, He accomplished that great and marvellous work, the overthrow of death, and all His other works.”

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: If all energy is defined as essential movement of some nature, as those who are versed in these matters say, where does one perceive any nature that has no movement, and is completely devoid of energy, or where does one find energy that is not movement of natural power?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But, as the blessed Cyril says, no one in his senses could admit that there was but one natural energy of God and His creation.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: It is not His human nature that raises up Lazarus from the dead, nor is it His divine power that sheds tears: for the shedding of tears is peculiar to human nature while the life is peculiar to the enhypostatic life.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But yet they are common the one to the other, because of the identity in subsistence.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For Christ is one, and one also is His person or subsistence, but yet He has two natures, one belonging to His humanity, and another belonging to His divinity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And the glory, indeed, which proceeded naturally from His divinity became common to both through the identity in subsistence, and again on account of His flesh that which was lowly became common to both.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For He Who is the one or the other, that is God or man, is one and the same, and both what is divine and what is human belong to Himself.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For while His divinity performed the miracles, they were not done apart from the flesh, and while His flesh performed its lowly offices, they were not done apart from the divinity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For His divinity was joined to the suffering flesh, yet remaining without passion, and endured the saving passions, and the holy mind was joined to the energising divinity of the Word, perceiving and knowing what was being accomplished.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And thus His divinity communicates its own glories to the body while it remains itself without part in the sufferings of the flesh.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For His flesh did not suffer through His divinity in the same way that His divinity energised through the flesh.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the flesh acted as the instrument of His divinity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Although, therefore, from the first conception there was no division at all between the two forms, but the actions of either form through all the time became those of one person, nevertheless we do not in any way confuse those things that took place without separation, but recognise from the quality of its works what sort of form anything has.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Christ, then, energises according to both His natures and either nature energises in Him in communion with the other, the Word performing through the authority and power of its divinity all the actions proper to the Word, i.e. all acts of supremacy and sovereignty, and the body performing all the actions proper to the body, in obedience to the will of the Word that is united to it, and of whom it has become a distinct part.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For He was not moved of Himself to the natural passions, nor again did He in that way recoil from the things of pain, and pray for release from them, or suffer what befel from without, but He was moved in conformity with His nature, the Word willing and allowing Him œconomically to suffer that, and to do the things proper to Him, that the truth might be confirmed by the works of nature.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Moreover, just as He received in His birth of a virgin superessential essence, so also He revealed His human energy in a superhuman way, walking with earthly feet on unstable water, not by turning the water into earth, but by causing it in the superabundant power of His divinity not to flow away nor yield beneath the weight of material feet.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For not in a merely human way did He do human things: for He was not only man, but also God, and so even His sufferings brought life and salvation: nor yet did He energise as God, strictly after the manner of God, for He was not only God, but also man, and so it was by touch and word and such like that He worked miracles.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But if any one should say, “We do not say that Christ has but one nature, in order to do away with His human energy, but we do so because human energy, in opposition to divine energy, is called passion (πάτθος),” we shall answer that, according to this reasoning, those also who hold that He has but one nature do not maintain this with a view to doing away with His human nature, but because human nature in opposition to divine nature is spoken of as passible (παθητική ).

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But God forbid that we should call the human activity passion, when we are distinguishing it from divine energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For, to speak generally, of nothing is the existence recognised or defined by comparison or collation.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: If it were so, indeed, existing things would turn out to be mutually the one the cause of the other.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For if the human activity is passion because the divine activity is energy, assuredly also the human nature must be wicked because the divine nature is good, and, by conversion and opposition, if the divine activity is called energy because the human activity is called passion, then also the divine nature must be good because the human nature is bad.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And so all created things must be bad, and he must have spoken falsely who said, And God saw every thing that He had made, and, behold, it was very good.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: We, therefore, maintain that the holy Fathers gave various names to the human activity according to the underlying notion.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For they called it power, and energy, and difference, and activity, and property, and quality, and passion, not in distinction from the divine activity, but power, because it is a conservative and invariable force; and energy, because it is a distinguishing mark, and reveals the absolute similarity between all things of the same class; and difference, because it distinguishes; and activity, because it makes manifest; and property, because it is constituent and belongs to that alone, and not to any other; and quality, because it gives form; and passion, because it is moved.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For all things that are of God and after God suffer in respect of being moved, forasmuch as they have not in themselves motion or power.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Therefore, as has been said, it is not in order to distinguish the one from the other that it has been named, but it is in accordance with the plan implanted in it in a creative manner by the Cause that framed the universe.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Wherefore, also, when they spoke of it along with the divine nature they called it energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For he who said, “For either form energises close communion with the other,” did something quite different from him who said, And when He had fasted forty days, He was afterwards an hungered: (for He allowed His nature to energise when it so willed, in the way proper to itself,) or from those who hold there is a different energy in Him or that He has a twofold energy, or now one energy and now another.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For these statements with the change in terms signify the two energies.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Indeed, often the number is indicated both by change of terms and by speaking of them as divine and human.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For the difference is difference in differing things, but how do things that do not exist differ?
